edge: PTQ is a technique used for compressing neural networks by converting full-precision models into quantized models using lower-precision data types.
PTQ helps reduce the size and computational cost of deep neural networks.

node_2: quantization noise and reduced prediction accuracy
edge: However, PTQ can introduce quantization noise and reduce prediction accuracy, especially in extremely low-bit settings.

node_2: scaling factors and rounding of weights
edge: The main problem is determining the appropriate quantization parameters like scaling factors and rounding of weights.
Existing methods determine quantization parameters by minimizing the distance between features before and after quantization.
PD-Quant addresses the limitation of only considering local information by incorporating global information in determining quantization parameters.
PD-Quant determines quantization parameters by utilizing the differences between network prediction before and after quantization.

node_2: overfitting problem alleviation and distribution adjustment
edge: PD-Quant addresses the overfitting problem in PTQ by adjusting the distribution of activations, improving quantization parameters and prediction accuracy in low-bit settings.
Experiments show that PD-Quant improves the prediction accuracy of models like ResNet-18 and RegNetX-600MF significantly in low-bit settings.
PD-Quant pushes the accuracy of ResNet-18 up to 53.14%
PD-Quant pushes the accuracy of RegNetX-600MF up to 40.67%

edge: Excellent works have been proposed to resolve the issues related to neural networks' memory footprint and inference latency, including knowledge distillation, model pruning, and model quantization.
Model quantization is an effective technique for compressing neural networks by keeping their weights and activations in low-bit data types to reduce memory and computation requirements.
A real-valued tensor x (weights or activations) can be mapped to the integer grid through a specific equation involving quantization and scaling factors.
Quantized models keep their weights and activations in low-bit data types to reduce memory and computation requirements.

The activation distribution of each FP block's input is corrected by achieving an approximate mean and variance with the BN statistics. This correction leads to better generalizability of the quantized model by DC.
The BN statistics come from the whole training set, and by correcting the distribution of activations, it becomes closer to the distribution of the entire training set.

PD and DC are the key components mentioned in the context that have a direct impact on the overall method. PD is often optimized with regularization to compensate for performance loss and alleviate overfitting, while DC is applied to improve the quantization parameters and alleviate overfitting problems in the baseline method.
PD-only optimized the quantization parameters by PD loss, but due to overfitting, Regularization is introduced to compensate for the performance loss and achieve higher accuracy than the baseline.

QDrop optimizes quantization parameters by computing the difference between quantized block activation and FP block activation. Our proposed DC can be applied to alleviate overfitting problems and improve performance.
QDrop can be enhanced by adding DC, resulting in an improvement of 0.9% at W2A2 compared to the baseline.
The introduction of DC in PD-Quant further improves performance by around 0.7% in MobileNetV2 at the W2A2 setting.
PD loss with regularization can significantly improve the accuracy of ResNet-18 by 1.68% and MobileNetV2 by 3.21% compared to the baseline at the W2A2 setting.

node_2: activation scaling factors and weight scaling factors
edge: The influence of PD loss on activation scaling factors and weight scaling factors was evaluated for ResNet-18. The experiments focused on optimizing only scaling factors to avoid the influence of other quantization parameters.
The optimization of scaling factors was done in the experiments to understand their impact on weight quantization.
All the existing methods, including PD loss, fail when weights are quantized with low bits.
An experiment was conducted on the 'First Layer' to study the impact of weight quantization where per-tensor weight quantization was set.
The method was effective in searching for optimal scaling factor during activation quantization, but not for weight quantization.
The parameter space of weights is deterministic, while the parameter space of activations varies with different input images.
